title: Asemeia marquesiana
intro: Asemeia marquesiana, also known as Marquesa's tickseed, is a plant species belonging to the Asteraceae family, commonly found in tropical and subtropical regions. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities, which may aid in the treatment of various ailments such as wounds, skin infections, and oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Asemeia marquesiana include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is often used in traditional medicine to prepare infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, and is sometimes included in commercial herbal products such as capsules and creams.
taxonomy: Asemeia marquesiana is classified within the family Malpighiaceae. This family, also known as the Barbados or Malpighia family, comprises approximately 133 species of plants, primarily found in the tropics and subtropics. Asemeia marquesiana is a species of flowering plant native to the island of São Tomé and Principe. The Malpighiaceae family is part of the larger order Malpighiales, which includes various flowering plants with distinct characteristics. Further classification within the Malpighiaceae family may be possible at the genus or species level.
